Article: Complex Instruction Following with Diverse Style Policies in Football Games (LCDSP)
Author: Chenglu Sun et al.
Method: Diverse Style Training modulates behaviour via continuous style params; a Style Interpreter maps free-form language to those params
Novelty: Language-controlled RL that follows abstract tactical STYLES ('prioritize defence, counterattack'), not just task outcomes
Others: Closest analogue to abstract intent ('focus on defense') in a real-time multi-agent team game
Evaluation Methods: Experiments in 5v5 football: abstract-instruction comprehension + diverse-style execution

Article: Adaptive Command: Real-Time Policy Adjustment via Language Models in StarCraft II
Author: Weiyu Ma et al.
Method: LLM strategic advisor + behaviour tree for execution + NL/speech interface to adjust SC2 strategy in real time
Novelty: Real-time language-driven COACH-MODE adjustment of an RTS policy; LLM reasons, behaviour tree executes
Result: — (significant gains reported; no numbers in abstract)
Others: Most direct precedent — NL real-time command of an SC2 policy
Evaluation Methods: User studies of human-AI real-time SC2 decision-making

Article: Natural Language-conditioned RL with Inside-out Task Language Development and Translation (TALAR)
Author: Jing-Cheng Pang et al. (Nanjing Univ.)
Method: Learn a predicate-based intermediate 'task language'; a translator maps free-form NL into it, decoupling comprehension from policy
Metric: Success rate; adaptability to unseen NL
Novelty: Compact task-language intermediate improves instruction-following + generalization to novel phrasings
Result: ~13.4% success-rate improvement over baselines
Others: Robustly grounding varied free-form phrasings into agent goals
Evaluation Methods: RL experiments: success + generalization to unseen instructions

Article: Building Open-Ended Embodied Agent via Language-Policy Bidirectional Adaptation (OpenPAL)
Author: Shaopeng Zhai et al.
Method: Two-stage co-training: fine-tune LLM to map free-form instructions to goals + goal-conditioned policy; align via bidirectional adaptation
Novelty: Instruction open-endedness: comprehends ARBITRARY free-form instructions (not a fixed set)
Result: — (no numbers in abstract)
Others: Open-vocabulary instruction->goal in a real-time game
Evaluation Methods: Tested in Contra FPS for comprehension + execution efficiency

Article: Scaling Instructable Agents Across Many Simulated Worlds (SIMA)
Author: SIMA Team, Google DeepMind (90+ authors)
Method: Train embodied agents to follow free-form language across many 3D worlds via a human-like interface (image+language in, keyboard/mouse out)
Novelty: Single agent grounds ARBITRARY language to perception+action across many 3D games
Result: — (preliminary; no headline numbers)
Others: Open-vocab real-time instruction following in commercial games — the interface paradigm we envision
Evaluation Methods: Real-time interaction across many 3D games via human-like I/O

Article: STEVE-1: A Generative Model for Text-to-Behavior in Minecraft
Author: Shalev Lifshitz et al. (Toronto / Vector)
Method: Instruction-tune VPT to follow commands in MineCLIP latent space, then train a text->latent prior; self-supervised BC + hindsight relabeling
Novelty: Instruction-tunes a generative behaviour model to follow open-ended text without large instruction-labeled trajectories (~$60 compute)
Result: Completes 12/13 early-game tasks; far outperforms prior baselines
Others: Open-ended text->low-level keyboard/mouse behaviour from raw pixels
Evaluation Methods: Early-game task-completion suite, low-level controls, pixel inputs

Article: Grounding Natural Language Commands to StarCraft II Game States for Narration-Guided RL
Author: Nicholas Waytowich et al. (US Army Research Lab)
Method: Learn a mutual-embedding projecting NL commands + SC2 goal-states into a shared space; 'narration' indicates sub-goals to densify reward
Novelty: Early demo of embedding free-form NL commands with high-dim RTS game states to guide RL under sparse reward
Others: Earliest precedent for grounding NL commands in SC2 for RL
Evaluation Methods: Embedding-based grounding used for narration-guided rewards in SC2

Article: RT-2: Vision-Language-Action Models Transfer Web Knowledge to Robotic Control
Author: Anthony Brohan et al. (Google DeepMind)
Method: Co-fine-tune a VLM on robot trajectories + web VQA; emit robot actions as text tokens from the same AR head (token-as-action)
Novelty: Single VLA folding action tokens into the language vocab; transfers web knowledge to closed-loop control
Result: 6,000 trials; improved generalization + unseen-command interpretation (exact numbers not on abstract)
Others: Canonical token-as-action VLA — encoding commands as action tokens
Evaluation Methods: Real-robot trials: generalization, semantic + multi-stage reasoning

Article: Do As I Can, Not As I Say: Grounding Language in Robotic Affordances (SayCan)
Author: Michael Ahn et al. (Google / Everyday Robots)
Method: LLM scores candidate skills for usefulness; a value function scores affordance (feasibility); product picks the next skill
Dataset: 101 real kitchen tasks; long-horizon abstract instructions
Novelty: Constrains LLM proposals to executable, feasible actions (LLM x affordance); LLM as high-level selector over a fixed skill set
Result: Affordance grounding ~doubles performance vs non-grounded (exact numbers not on abstract)
Others: Cleanest template: abstract intent ('focus on defense') -> constrained high-level skill selection
Evaluation Methods: Real-robot execution of long-horizon NL instructions

Article: Code as Policies: Language Model Programs for Embodied Control
Author: Jacky Liang et al. (Google Robotics)
Method: Prompt a code LLM to write Python policy code (loops, conditionals, API calls) from NL; hierarchical code-gen defines new functions on the fly
Novelty: Code-as-action: the LLM output is an executable program over an API; generalization, interpretability, reactive logic without extra training
Result: 39.8% on HumanEval; working reactive + waypoint policies on multiple robots
Others: 'LLM emits programs/API calls as actions' — apt for structured macro-commands to crowds
Evaluation Methods: HumanEval + real/sim robot task demos

Article: OpenVLA: An Open-Source Vision-Language-Action Model
Author: Moo Jin Kim et al. (Stanford)
Method: 7B VLA = Llama 2 + fused DINOv2/SigLIP; predicts discretized action tokens autoregressively; LoRA fine-tuning
Novelty: Open, efficiently fine-tunable token-as-action VLA beating much larger closed models with 7x fewer params
Result: +16.5% abs success over RT-2-X (55B) on 29 tasks; +20.4% over Diffusion Policy; 7x fewer params
Others: Open reference token-emitting LLM controller; recipe transfers to a game action space
Evaluation Methods: Multi-embodiment real-robot success across 29 tasks

Article: Octo: An Open-Source Generalist Robot Policy
Author: Octo Model Team (Dibya Ghosh et al.)
Method: Transformer diffusion policy conditioned on language OR goal images; flexible task/observation tokens; finetunes to new sensors/actions
Novelty: Open generalist policy with a DIFFUSION action head (not token-as-action) + modular conditioning
Result: Evaluated on 9 platforms; finetunes in hours on consumer GPUs (no numeric scores on abstract)
Others: Contrast point: continuous/diffusion action head vs token-as-action for the executor
Evaluation Methods: Real-robot finetune + success on 9 platforms; ablations

Article: pi0: A Vision-Language-Action Flow Model for General Robot Control
Author: Kevin Black et al. (Physical Intelligence)
Method: Flow-matching 'action expert' on a pretrained VLM; iteratively denoises action chunks into continuous high-frequency (up to 50 Hz) actions
Novelty: Flow-matching action expert on a VLM: continuous high-frequency control keeping internet-scale semantics
Result: Demonstrates laundry folding, table bussing, box assembly; beats baselines (numbers not on abstract)
Others: The 'action expert bolted to an LLM/VLM' pattern; real-time execution under latency
Evaluation Methods: Real-robot zero-shot + finetuned dexterous tasks

Article: FAST: Efficient Action Tokenization for Vision-Language-Action Models
Author: Karl Pertsch et al. (Physical Intelligence)
Method: Compress action chunks with a DCT then byte-pair-encode them (frequency-space tokenization); release universal tokenizer FAST+
Dataset: 10,000 h robot data; FAST+ on 1M trajectories
Metric: Training time; success vs diffusion VLAs
Novelty: Frequency-space action tokenization fixes lossy per-dim/per-step binning, letting AR VLAs match diffusion VLAs
Result: Trains up to 5x faster than diffusion VLAs while matching performance at scale
Others: Directly about HOW to discretize a continuous action space into LLM tokens — key for a token-emitting commander
Evaluation Methods: Training-efficiency + success vs diffusion VLAs

Article: Fast-in-Slow: A Dual-System Foundation Model Unifying Fast Manipulation within Slow Reasoning
Author: Hao Chen et al.
Method: Embed a fast System-1 executor inside a VLM System-2 reasoner via partial parameter sharing; asynchronous frequencies (1:4), dual-aware co-training
Novelty: Single model unifying slow reasoning + fast control (vs two models); executor reuses the LLM's latent conditions
Result: 117.7 Hz on RTX 4090 (chunk 8); +8% sim and +11% real success over prior SOTA
Others: Dual-system fast/slow = the latency-vs-reasoning trade-off central to real-time crowd command
Evaluation Methods: Sim + real manipulation success + control-frequency benchmarking

Article: CombatVLA: An Efficient VLA Model for Combat Tasks in 3D Action Role-Playing Games
Author: Peng Chen et al.
Method: 3B VLA trained on video-action pairs as 'action-of-thought' (AoT) sequences; truncated-AoT for fast inference in real-time combat
Dataset: Video-action pairs (action tracker) + a combat-understanding benchmark
Novelty: VLA specialized for real-time game combat via action-of-thought reasoning + truncation for second-level latency
Result: 50x acceleration; success exceeding human players; outperforms all existing models on the combat benchmark
Others: Closest prior art — a VLA acting in a real-time game under latency (single-agent, not crowd command)
Evaluation Methods: Combat-understanding benchmark + in-game success + speed

Article: Divide and Conquer: Grounding LLMs as Efficient Decision-Making Agents via Offline Hierarchical RL (GLIDER)
Author: Zican Hu et al.
Method: Two-layer hierarchy: high-level LLM emits chain-of-thought sub-task goals; low-level controller executes primitives; trained with offline hierarchical RL
Novelty: Grounds an LLM as a high-level decision-maker by decomposing into coherent sub-tasks supervising a low-level controller
Result: — (consistent gains; no numbers on abstract)
Others: Hierarchical LLM-sets-goals / policy-executes -> maps to a commander LLM steering unit policies
Evaluation Methods: Agent benchmarks (ScienceWorld, ALFWorld)

Article: BabyAI: A Platform to Study the Sample Efficiency of Grounded Language Learning
Author: Maxime Chevalier-Boisvert, Dzmitry Bahdanau et al. (Mila)
Method: Gridworld + heuristic bot expert for instruction-following RL/IL; agent gets state + synthetic-language instruction
Dataset: BabyAI platform — 19 levels, compositional English subset
Novelty: Controlled, extensible benchmark for the sample efficiency of grounded language learning, with a built-in expert
Result: — (shows deep methods not yet sample-efficient; numbers not in abstract)
Others: Canonical testbed for the concrete-order side of NL command following; close to a command-arena warm-up
Evaluation Methods: Per-level success vs amount of supervision; curriculum

Article: Language as an Abstraction for Hierarchical Deep Reinforcement Learning
Author: Yiding Jiang et al. (Stanford / Google Brain; Chelsea Finn)
Method: High-level policy issues NL instructions as subgoals to a language-conditioned low-level policy; language's compositionality is the abstraction layer
Novelty: SEMINAL: language as a compositional abstraction between high- and low-level policies -> combinatorial generalization
Result: — (superior systematic generalization vs non-compositional; no single number)
Others: Seminal grounding for the two-tier design: abstract command on top, unit policies below
Evaluation Methods: Task-performance + compositional-generalization study

Article: Grounding Large Language Models in Interactive Environments with Online RL (GLAM)
Author: Thomas Carta, Clement Romac et al. (Inria Flowers / HF; Oudeyer)
Method: Use an LLM directly as the action policy; update it with online RL (PPO) through environment interaction to functionally ground its knowledge
Novelty: An LLM can be turned into a grounded interactive agent via online RL, improving sample efficiency + generalization
Result: — (positive results; no numbers in abstract)
Others: On-point recipe to make an LLM command-follower learn by acting — template for LLM-as-commander policy
Evaluation Methods: Online RL in textual env; sample efficiency + generalization

Article: Eureka: Human-Level Reward Design via Coding Large Language Models
Author: Yecheng Jason Ma et al. (UPenn / NVIDIA)
Method: GPT-4 evolutionary search over executable reward-function code from env source + task description, with reflective improvement
Dataset: 29 RL envs, 10 robot morphologies (Isaac Gym); Shadow Hand pen-spinning
Metric: % tasks beating human rewards; normalized improvement
Novelty: LLMs can author reward functions surpassing human experts; gradient-free in-context RLHF folds in NL feedback
Result: Outperforms human-designed rewards on 83% of tasks; ~52% avg normalized improvement
Others: Route from abstract language intent ('be aggressive') to executable reward signals
Evaluation Methods: Train RL on generated rewards; compare to human rewards across 29 tasks

Article: Text2Reward: Reward Shaping with Language Models for RL
Author: Tianbao Xie, Siheng Zhao et al. (HKU / XLANG)
Method: Data-free: prompt an LLM to generate dense interpretable reward code over a compact env representation, with iterative human-feedback refinement
Novelty: Generates interpretable DENSE shaped rewards (not sparse) from language, refinable via human feedback, sim-to-real
Result: Matches/beats human-oracle reward on 13/17 manipulation tasks; 6 novel locomotion behaviours at >94% success
Others: Language -> dense reward with human-in-the-loop; coach-mode intent shaping behaviour
Evaluation Methods: Train RL (PPO/SAC) on generated rewards; success vs oracle; sim-to-real

Article: Benchmarking the Spectrum of Agent Capabilities (Crafter)
Author: Danijar Hafner (Toronto / Google Brain)
Method: Crafter: a fast procedurally-generated 2D open-world survival game scoring 22 semantic achievements in one env
Novelty: Consolidates many general abilities (exploration, long-horizon, generalization) into one efficient benchmark
Result: — (baseline scores for reward + unsupervised agents; numbers not in abstract)
Others: Lightweight open-ended RL benchmark on the complexity ladder; prototyping before SC2
Evaluation Methods: Train within a step budget; achievement-based score

Article: Language Grounded Multi-agent RL with Human-interpretable Communication (LangGround)
Author: Huao Li et al. (Univ. Pittsburgh; Honda RI; CMU - Katia Sycara)
Method: Align MARL agents' learned communication with a human-language embedding space by grounding messages on synthetic data from embodied LLM agents
Metric: Task performance; comm emergence; zero-shot teammates
Novelty: Makes emergent MARL communication human-interpretable while accelerating emergence + ad-hoc teamwork
Result: — (improves interpretability + zero-shot ad-hoc teamwork; no numbers in abstract)
Models: MARL + comm; embodied LLMs for grounding
Others: Multi-agent coordination via human language — the 'command crowds' core, interpretable for a human commander
Evaluation Methods: Train language-grounded MARL; compare to emergent-comm; zero-shot unseen teammates

Article: Towards Language-Augmented Multi-Agent Deep RL
Author: Maxime Toquebiau, Nicolas Bredeche (Sorbonne / ISIR)
Method: Train MARL agents to act AND produce/interpret human-defined NL descriptions of observations; language grounding for comm + representation learning
Metric: Task performance; representation quality; partner generalization
Novelty: Human-defined language as a grounding signal that doubles as a comm protocol + representation objective
Result: — (outperforms emergent-comm baselines; no numbers in abstract)
Others: Recent multi-agent+language recipe; grounding crowd-level comm in human command vocab
Evaluation Methods: Compare to emergent-comm on performance, partner generalization, human-agent interaction

Article: Improving Multimodal Interactive Agents with RL from Human Feedback
Author: Josh Abramson, Arun Ahuja et al. (DeepMind Interactive Agents)
Method: RLHF for embodied, language-instructable agents in a 3D world; an Inter-temporal Bradley-Terry (IBT) reward model captures moment-to-moment progress
Novelty: RLHF (with a temporal reward model) for multimodal language-conditioned embodied agents, no programmatic rewards
Result: — (improved on all metrics vs imitation; no numbers in abstract)
Others: RLHF-for-control: shaping a language-instructed agent from human preference; coach-mode feedback
Evaluation Methods: Reward model from human progress annotations; RL-optimize; live human judgment

Article: TextStarCraft II: Large Language Models Play StarCraft II (Chain of Summarization)
Author: Weiyu Ma et al.
Method: Text wrapper on SC2 (python-sc2): Observation-to-Text + Text-to-Action adapters; LLM agents play full games vs built-in AI
Dataset: Interactive SC2 env + 5 knowledge-probe categories; text in/out
Metric: Win rate vs built-in AI (6 levels); human strategy assessment
Novelty: First benchmark of LLMs as real-time SC2 commanders via pure NL; Chain-of-Summarization for long-horizon observation compression
Result: GPT-3.5-16k vs Zerg AI: VeryEasy 8/8, Easy 9/9, Medium 8/8, Hard 21/25, Harder 7/14, VeryHard 0/12
Others: CLOSEST analogue: free-form text commands -> SC2 macro actions at game speed; adapter design is a direct reference
Evaluation Methods: Win-rate vs built-in AI per difficulty; human knowledge eval

Article: TacticCraft: Natural Language-Driven Tactical Adaptation for StarCraft II
Author: Weiyu Ma
Method: Adapter modules on each action head conditioned on a 'tactical tensor' of strategic preferences; KL constraints preserve base competence
Novelty: Targets ABSTRACT tactical intent ('be aggressive', 'expand', 'tech up') -> coach-mode steering of a frozen SC2 policy
Others: Maps directly onto our 'abstract intent / focus on defense' for a crowd-controlling SC2 policy
Evaluation Methods: Behavioural eval across tactical dimensions while checking skill retention

Article: SC2EGSet: StarCraft II Esport Replay and Game-state Dataset
Author: Andrzej Bialecki et al.
Method: Parse 55 tournament replaypacks (since 2016) into cleaned per-game game-state; open PyTorch / Lightning loaders
Dataset: 55 replaypacks -> 17,930 game-state files (replay + structured state); no language channel
Novelty: Large, fully parsed, public SC2 esport dataset aligned to game state, with reproducible ML tooling
Others: Canonical SC2 replay/state substrate to LAYER language commands/commentary onto
Evaluation Methods: — (provides loaders, not a scored task)

Article: Game-MUG: Multimodal Game Situation Understanding and Commentary Generation Dataset
Author: Zhihao Zhang et al.
Method: Collect LoL live streams (2020-2022) pairing match event logs, caster transcripts, and audience chat; joint multimodal dual-learning model
Dataset: 70k clips + transcripts; 3.7M audience chats; 45 streams; 216 matches; 15k events; 3 leagues
Novelty: Aligns caster commentary AND live audience chat to discrete in-game events on a shared timeline
Result: — (numbers not on abstract; PDF unparseable)
Others: Coaching/commentary analogue: NL time-aligned to game events (MOBA)
Evaluation Methods: Game-situation/event understanding + commentary generation

Article: LoL-V2T: Large-Scale Esports Video Description Dataset
Author: Tsunehiko Tanaka, Edgar Simo-Serra (Waseda)
Method: LoL Worlds footage (157 matches) segmented into clips, paired with captions from manual + ASR subtitles
Dataset: 9,723 clips; 62,677 captions; 157 LoL Worlds matches; video + EN captions
Novelty: Largest video-game video-description dataset at release; jargon-heavy vocab + event-to-commentary temporal lag
Result: — (no SOTA numbers on page)
Others: Gameplay video -> NL; the event-to-caption LAG is the same alignment problem we face
Evaluation Methods: Video-captioning eval on held-out captions; splits provided

Article: SCBench: A Sports Commentary Benchmark for Video LLMs
Author: Kuangzhi Ge et al. (Peking Univ.)
Method: Short clips across 6 sports, each with professional EN commentary + 6 semantic dimension labels; GPT-based multi-dimensional scoring
Dataset: 5,775 clips (4,908 train / 867 test); 6 sports; avg 14.5 s; video + timestamped commentary
Metric: SCORES: 6 dims incl. Tactical Analysis, Match-Situation Interpretation
Novelty: First benchmark to evaluate Video LLMs on professional sports commentary, with an explicit TACTICAL-ANALYSIS dimension
Result: — (relative model rankings; no single SOTA number)
Baselines: 8 zero-shot Video LLMs (VILA, Video-LLaVA, LongVA, LLaVA-NeXT-Video, Kangaroo, Chat-UniVi-1.5, InternVL-Chat-2, Mini-InternVL)
Others: 'Tactical Analysis' / 'Match Situation' dims = coach-mode language; a template for scoring commentary outputs
Evaluation Methods: GPT-based scoring on 6 SCORES dimensions vs ground-truth

Article: GOAL: Knowledge-grounded Video Captioning for Real-time Soccer Commentary Generation
Author: Ji Qi et al. (THU-KEG, Tsinghua)
Method: Knowledge-Grounded Video Captioning from soccer broadcast clips, pairing each clip with commentary + a background knowledge base of triples
Dataset: 8,900+ soccer clips; 22k sentences; 42k knowledge triples; video + text + triples
Novelty: Knowledge-grounded commentary: caption must reason over an external knowledge graph, not just describe pixels; targets real-time
Result: — (per-model numbers not extracted)
Others: Knowledge-grounding matches our need to tie language to domain facts (unit/ability/map knowledge)
Evaluation Methods: Caption-quality vs ground-truth with knowledge triples

Article: VC_NBA_2022: Knowledge Guided Entity-aware Video Captioning (Basketball Benchmark)
Author: Zeyu Xi et al.
Method: Build a basketball knowledge graph (KG_NBA_2022), then a captioning dataset; an entity-aware captioner conditions on a candidate-player list
Dataset: 9 fine-grained shooting-event types; 286 players' knowledge; video + entity knowledge + captions
Novelty: Entity-aware sports commentary: text must name the correct specific players + fine-grained actions
Result: — (outperforms advanced captioners; specific numbers not on abstract)
Others: Entity-aware generation parallels referring to specific units/groups when commanding/narrating a crowd
Evaluation Methods: Entity-aware captioning vs ground-truth; player-identity correctness

Article: MineDojo: Building Open-Ended Embodied Agents with Internet-Scale Knowledge
Author: Jim Fan, Guanzhi Wang et al. (NVIDIA / Caltech / Stanford)
Method: Minecraft agent framework + internet knowledge; MineCLIP (video-text contrastive on time-aligned YouTube narration) is a learned language-conditioned reward
Dataset: 730k+ narrated YouTube videos (2.2B words); ~7k wiki; 340k Reddit; 1000s language-prompted tasks
Novelty: Internet-scale time-aligned language<->video grounding -> follow free-form NL tasks without hand-designed dense rewards
Result: — (MineCLIP-reward agents solve diverse tasks; rates not extracted)
Baselines: Manually-shaped reward vs learned MineCLIP reward
Others: Strongest large-scale precedent for grounding free-form language via time-aligned text; model for mining language<->action pairs from replays+commentary
Evaluation Methods: Programmatic + learned-reward eval on the language-prompted task suite

Article: IGLU / IDAT: Interactive Grounded Language Understanding in a Collaborative Environment
Author: Julia Kiseleva et al. (comp.); Shrestha Mohanty et al. (IDAT) (Microsoft Research)
Method: Architect issues NL building instructions (with clarification questions); Builder executes block placements; each instruction logged with action states
Dataset: Full set: 8,136 instruction-action pairs; 1,056 clarification cases (~9,000 utterances); 11x11x9 voxel; 230 annotators
Novelty: Pairs NL instructions (+ clarification dialogue) with grounded DISCRETE construction actions, incl. ambiguous/abstract intent
Result: — (competition; scores vary by entrant)
Others: Closest 'NL -> discrete agent actions' with ambiguous-intent clarification; architect/builder mirrors commander/crowd
Evaluation Methods: Human-in-the-loop + automatic structure-match; instruction<->block-placement logs

Article: CrafText: Instruction-following Benchmark over Crafter (partially verified)
Author: (authors not confirmed)
Method: NL-instruction tasks over the Crafter env across Localization / Conditional / Building / Achievement task types
Dataset: ~3,924 NL instructions over Crafter (size confirmed from abstract; metrics/baselines NOT verified)
Novelty: Synthetic instruction->action benchmark complementing BabyAI/Crafter with richer language
Others: Synthetic command->action complement; FLAGGED partially verified — re-check metrics/baselines before citing
Evaluation Methods: — (not confirmed)

Article: MotionStreamer: Streaming Motion Generation via Diffusion-based Autoregressive Model in Causal Latent Space
Author: Lixing Xiao et al. (ZJU)
Method: Causal Temporal AutoEncoder (1D causal convs, downsample 4) into a continuous causal latent; 12-layer AR Transformer + diffusion head predicts next latents online
Metric: FID, R-Precision, MM-Dist, Diversity, first-frame latency
Novelty: Continuous (non-discretized) causal latents cut info loss + error accumulation; enables streaming generation
Result: HumanML3D FID 10.724 (272-dim protocol); R@1 0.631 / R@3 0.851; Causal TAE lowest first-frame latency (no ms/FPS in tables)
Baselines: MDM, MLD, T2M-GPT, MotionGPT, MoMask, AttT2M, FlowMDM
Models: Causal TAE + diffusion-head AR Transformer; T5-XXL text
Others: Streaming/online text-to-motion (your named paper); the causal-latent design
Evaluation Methods: Quantitative FID/R@k on HumanML3D + first-frame latency

Article: MotionBricks: Scalable Real-Time Motions with Modular Latent Generative Model and Smart Primitives
Author: Tingwu Wang et al. (+15 incl. Xue Bin Peng) (NVIDIA / NVlabs)
Method: Large modular latent generative backbone trained on 350k+ clips in one model; 'smart primitives' give a plug-and-play velocity/heading/style command interface, no retraining
Novelty: Real-time scalable generation + a smart-primitives authoring interface; one unified backbone for 350k+ skills
Result: ~15,000 FPS at ~2 ms latency from one model; demoed on Unitree G1 humanoid
Baselines: — (SOTA claim; no baseline list in abstract)
Models: Modular latent generative model + smart-primitive interface
Others: Your named paper; the real-time + crowd-skill scaling angle for embodiment
Evaluation Methods: — (needs full paper)

Article: MoMask: Generative Masked Modeling of 3D Human Motions
Author: Chuan Guo et al. (incl. Li Cheng, Univ. Alberta Vision & Learning Lab)
Method: Hierarchical residual VQ tokens generated by a bidirectional masked transformer (base) + a residual transformer; iterative parallel token prediction
Novelty: Residual/hierarchical quantization + generative masked modeling for high-fidelity editable text-to-motion; parallel (non-AR) decoding
Result: HumanML3D FID 0.045 (vs 0.141 T2M-GPT); KIT-ML FID 0.228 (vs 0.514); ~0.081 s/motion
Models: Residual VQ-VAE + Masked + Residual Transformer; CLIP
Others: LI CHENG (supervisor) is a co-author; near-real-time parallel decoding
Evaluation Methods: Quantitative metrics on HumanML3D/KIT-ML vs token + diffusion baselines

Article: MotionLCM: Real-time Controllable Motion Generation via Latent Consistency Model
Author: Wenxun Dai et al.
Method: Distill a motion latent diffusion model into a latent consistency model for 1-4 step inference; motion ControlNet in latent space
Novelty: First latent-consistency formulation for motion -> controllable text-to-motion in real time with one-step sampling
Result: 1-step AITS 0.030 s (>30 FPS); 1929x faster than OmniControl, 13x faster than MLD; 1-step FID 0.467, R-Prec 0.803
Baselines: MLD, MotionDiffuse, MDM, T2M, TEMOS, OmniControl
Models: VAE latent + latent consistency model + motion ControlNet
Others: Explicitly real-time (one-step); strongest 'language-conditioned motion under a compute budget' reference
Evaluation Methods: FID/R-Prec on HumanML3D + runtime (AITS) vs diffusion baselines

Article: DartControl (DART): A Diffusion-Based Autoregressive Motion Model for Real-Time Text-Driven Motion Control
Author: Kaifeng Zhao et al. (ETH Zurich)
Method: Compact latent motion-primitive space conditioned on motion history + text; autoregressively denoise primitives (10 steps) for online streaming; spatial goals via latent optimization or RL
Novelty: Real-time ONLINE text-driven motion control combining AR motion primitives + latent diffusion; streaming text + spatial constraints without paired data
Result: 300 FPS on one RTX 4090 (240 FPS w/ RL); first-frame latency 0.02 s; ~10x faster than FlowMDM; segment FID 3.79 (vs 5.81)
Baselines: FlowMDM, TEACH, DoubleTake, T2M-GPT, DNO, OmniControl, GAMMA
Models: VAE primitive compression + latent diffusion (DDIM 10) + CLIP
Others: Real-time AND streaming (online text stream); closest match to 'streaming AR text-to-motion under latency'
Evaluation Methods: Composition/in-between benchmarks + runtime + human preference

Article: T2M-GPT: Generating Human Motion from Textual Descriptions with Discrete Representations
Author: Jianrong Zhang et al.
Method: CNN VQ-VAE discretizes motion into tokens (EMA + code reset); a GPT transformer autoregressively generates tokens from text
Novelty: Shows a simple discrete-token VQ-VAE + GPT pipeline beats contemporaneous diffusion methods
Result: HumanML3D FID 0.116 (vs MotionDiffuse 0.630); R-Precision comparable to best
Others: Seminal AR token-based motion model; the lineage MotionStreamer/MotionGPT build on
Evaluation Methods: FID/R-Precision on HumanML3D + KIT-ML

Article: MotionGPT: Human Motion as a Foreign Language
Author: Biao Jiang et al. (Fudan / Tencent)
Method: VQ-VAE motion tokens + a unified motion-language model (T5) on mixed motion-text data; instruction/prompt-based QA across tasks
Novelty: Treats motion as a language modality in one LLM, unifying generation, captioning, prediction, in-betweening
Result: — (SOTA across tasks; per-task numbers not extracted)
Others: Discrete-token LLM-style motion model; 'command-as-language' framing aligns with our NL spine
Evaluation Methods: Multi-task eval on HumanML3D/KIT-ML

Article: CrowdMoGen: Event-Driven Collective Human Motion Generation (Zero-Shot Text-Driven Collective Motion)
Author: Yukang Cao et al. (NTU S-Lab)
Method: Two-stage zero-shot: an LLM Crowd Scene Planner groups individuals + assigns event-aligned plans; a transformer Collective Motion Generator (SMPL priors) synthesizes the crowd
Novelty: First zero-shot framework for COLLECTIVE/CROWD motion from text — multi-person interaction, large-scale dynamics, scene arrangement
Result: — (three crowd-motion types demoed; no numbers)
Others: SINGLE most on-theme for crowd-motion embodiment; explicitly motivated by games + urban sim
Evaluation Methods: Qualitative crowd scenarios; LLM-planned scene coherence

Article: Towards Open Domain Text-Driven Synthesis of Multi-Person Motions
Author: Mengyi Shan et al.
Method: MDM-style transformer diffusion with interleaved pose/motion layers; trained jointly on heterogeneous + 2 new pseudo-labeled datasets for variable-count multi-person motion
Dataset: LAION-Pose (8M, new), WebVid-Motion (3,500, new), HumanML3D, InterHuman
Novelty: First open-domain text-to-MULTI-PERSON motion; scales beyond fixed two-person setups via web-mined supervision
Result: Up to 61 frames / 10 subjects; Pose FID 1.329, Motion FID 1.220, Pose R-Prec@1 0.539
Others: Multi-character (up to 10, any count) text-driven — complements CrowdMoGen on the crowd axis
Evaluation Methods: Pose/Motion FID + R-Precision vs multi-person baselines

Article: Light-T2M: A Lightweight and Fast Model for Text-to-Motion Generation
Author: Ling-An Zeng et al. (Sun Yat-sen Univ.)
Method: Diffusion text-to-motion combining lightweight convolutions (local) + Mamba state-space blocks (global) + an Adaptive Textual Information Injector
Metric: FID, R-Precision, MM-Dist, inference time, param count
Novelty: Extreme parameter/compute efficiency: ~10% of MoMask's params while improving quality + speed
Result: 4.48M params (vs 44.85M MoMask); 0.152 s/sample (vs 0.180 s); HumanML3D FID 0.040; KIT-ML FID 0.161
Models: Lightweight CNN + Mamba (SSM); adaptive text injector
Others: Language-conditioned motion under a compute/memory budget — fits our latency/memory framing
Evaluation Methods: FID/R-Prec on HumanML3D/KIT-ML + params + wall-clock

Article: Feudal Multi-Agent Hierarchies for Cooperative Reinforcement Learning
Author: Sanjeevan Ahilan, Peter Dayan (Gatsby / MPI)
Method: A 'manager' learns to communicate subgoals (messages defining worker reward functions) to multiple workers acting simultaneously
Novelty: Feudal hierarchy for cooperative MARL: a high-level commander sets per-worker goals via communication, reducing non-stationarity
Result: — (scales better than shared-reward cooperative baselines)
Others: Seminal commander->many-workers goal-setting hierarchy; the pattern we generalize to language
Evaluation Methods: Reward + scaling vs shared-reward baselines

Article: Hierarchical reinforcement learning with natural language subgoals
Author: Arun Ahuja et al. (DeepMind / NYU)
Method: A high-level policy issues natural-language subgoals to a low-level policy in a 3D embodied env; subgoal space softly supervised by human data
Novelty: Unconstrained natural language as the high-level->low-level subgoal interface, parameterized from human data (not a fixed goal set)
Result: — (beats BC and HRL-from-scratch)
Others: Direct model for 'abstract language command -> low-level execution' (single-agent)
Evaluation Methods: vs BC + unsupervised-HRL on long-range embodied tasks

Article: Hierarchical Multi-Agent Skill Discovery (HMASD)
Author: Mingyu Yang et al. (USTC)
Method: Two-level: a transformer 'skill coordinator' assigns one team skill + per-agent skills; a low-level 'skill discoverer' picks actions conditioned on the skill
Novelty: Jointly discovers team-level + individual skills, coordinated by a transformer high-level controller (a learned commander)
Result: — (significant gains over strong MARL baselines)
Others: Closest RL-native analogue of a commander assigning coordinated goals to many agents
Evaluation Methods: vs MARL baselines on sparse-reward cooperative tasks

Article: Language Instructed Reinforcement Learning for Human-AI Coordination (instructRL)
Author: Hengyuan Hu, Dorsa Sadigh (Stanford)
Method: A pretrained LLM generates a prior policy from a human NL instruction; the prior regularizes the RL objective toward a human-preferred equilibrium
Novelty: Natural-language instruction selects among multiple equilibria in cooperative RL — language steers which convention agents adopt
Result: — (language significantly boosts human-AI coordination in Hanabi)
Others: Language as the lever to align coordination with the commander's intent
Evaluation Methods: Self-play + human eval on Hanabi

Article: SwarmBrain: Embodied agent for real-time strategy game StarCraft II via LLMs
Author: Xiao Shao et al.
Method: Two-tier: an LLM 'Overmind' sets macro strategy; a 'Swarm ReflexNet' state machine handles low-latency unit tactics, compensating for LLM latency
Novelty: LLM-orchestrated macro command + reactive reflex layer for a real-time RTS, engineered around the LLM latency tradeoff
Result: Victories across difficulties (secondary source: 100% to Medium-Hard, 76% Hard — UNCONFIRMED on primary page)
Others: Direct precedent: high-level intent -> coordinated army in real-time RTS under LLM latency
Evaluation Methods: Live matches vs built-in AI at multiple difficulties

Article: LLM-PySC2: StarCraft II Learning Environment for Large Language Models
Author: Zongyuan Li et al. (NKAI Decision Team)
Method: Env exposing the full pysc2 action space to LLM agents (multimodal obs + game-wiki knowledge); native multi-agent via an async query architecture keeping latency consistent with population size
Dataset: StarCraft II / pysc2; SMAC + new macro/micro scenarios
Novelty: First env giving LLMs the complete pysc2 action space with native multi-agent control; async design targets real-time scalability
Result: — (LLMs show potential but struggle with hallucinations + inefficient collaboration without instructions)
Others: Multi-agent LLM commander testbed with explicit latency-vs-scale handling — near-exact Phase-1 wedge (already vendored in our bench repo)
Evaluation Methods: Macro-decision + micro-operation SC2 scenarios, multi-agent

Article: ICCO: Learning an Instruction-conditioned Coordinator for Language-guided Task-aligned Multi-robot Control
Author: Yoshiki Yano et al. (NAIST)
Method: MARL with one Coordinator + multiple Local Agents; the Coordinator fuses instruction + state into Task-Aligned Consistent Instructions, trained with a task+adherence reward + a mutual-information consistency term
Novelty: A learned instruction-conditioned coordinator between one language command and many robots, enforcing task-alignment + consistency
Others: Centralized-command / decentralized-execution with language — the single-command -> coordinated many-agent interface
Evaluation Methods: Sim + real-world multi-robot experiments

Article: Language-Driven Coordination and Learning in Multi-Agent Simulation Environments (LLM-MARL)
Author: Zhengyang Li et al.
Method: Integrate LLMs into MARL via Coordinator (subgoals), Communicator (symbolic messaging), Memory (episodic recall); PPO + a language-conditioned loss + LLM-query gating
Dataset: Google Research Football, MAgent Battle, StarCraft II
Metric: Win rate; coordination score; zero-shot generalization
Novelty: Unified LLM-MARL stack: LLM subgoals + symbolic comm drive coordination; gating limits costly LLM queries (real-time)
Result: — (consistent improvements over MAPPO and QMIX)
Others: Coordinator issues subgoals to many agents on RTS-style benchmarks incl. SC2 + MAgent (large populations)
Evaluation Methods: Win rate / coordination / zero-shot vs MAPPO + QMIX

Article: SMAC-Talk: A Natural Language Extension of the StarCraft Multi-Agent Challenge for LLMs
Author: Sol & Najjaran
Method: Adds a natural-language communication channel over SMAC, with an embedded deceptive communicator; benchmarks LLM agents
Novelty: NL-MARL StarCraft benchmark with a deceptive-communication element
Others: A 2026 NL-MARL StarCraft benchmark — directly our command-of-units-via-language setting
Evaluation Methods: SMAC tasks with a natural-language channel

Article: OmniControl: Control Any Joint at Any Time for Human Motion Generation
Author: Yiming Xie et al. (Northeastern)
Method: Text-conditioned motion diffusion + analytic spatial guidance + a ControlNet-style realism branch enforcing arbitrary joint constraints
Novelty: First single model to accept flexible spatial control over ANY joint at ANY time (not just pelvis)
Result: ~FID 0.218, avg error ~3.38 cm (pelvis, via TLControl comparison); ~1.6 FPS (slow)
Others: Steered by spatial keypoint constraints on any joint subset at any time
Evaluation Methods: HumanML3D/KIT-ML FID/R-Prec + per-joint error + ablations

Article: Guided Motion Diffusion for Controllable Human Motion Synthesis (GMD)
Author: Korrawe Karunratanakul et al. (ETH Zurich)
Method: Motion diffusion with feature-projection + dense-guidance turning sparse keyframe/trajectory signals into dense guidance
Novelty: Feature projection coupling spatial info with poses + dense guidance for reliable sparse-constraint satisfaction
Result: — (improves over T2M SOTA while enabling spatial control; ~1.8 FPS)
Models: Diffusion over root trajectory + motion, guidance
Others: Steered by global trajectory, sparse keyframes, obstacle/goal functions
Evaluation Methods: HumanML3D FID/R-Prec + task-specific control error

Article: TLControl: Trajectory and Language Control for Human Motion Synthesis
Author: Weilin Wan et al. (HKU / UPenn)
Method: Body-part VQ-VAE latent + a Masked Trajectories Transformer for coarse estimation, then test-time optimization to refine to exact trajectories
Novelty: Joint low-level trajectory + high-level language control via hybrid neural+optimization; precise multi-joint control at interactive speed
Result: Pelvis FID 0.271, avg error 1.08 cm (vs OmniControl 3.38); runs 61.7 FPS (~38x faster)
Others: Steered by partial multi-joint trajectories + text; REAL-TIME (61.7 FPS)
Evaluation Methods: HumanML3D/KIT-ML realism + control error + runtime

Article: Generating Human Interaction Motions in Scenes with Text Control (TeSMo)
Author: Hongwei Yi et al. (NVIDIA / MPI)
Method: Two-stage diffusion: a scene-agnostic goal-reaching text-to-motion model, then a scene-aware branch fine-tuned with floor + object geometry
Novelty: Brings text controllability + scene/object awareness into kinematic motion diffusion
Result: — (diverse plausible human-scene interactions; no numbers in abstract)
Others: Steered by text + start/goal positions + scene geometry
Evaluation Methods: — (qualitative; protocol not in abstract)

Article: Flexible Motion In-betweening with Diffusion Models (CondMDI)
Author: Setareh Cohan et al. (UBC; van de Panne)
Method: Masked conditional motion diffusion trained on random keyframes + random joints, with an observed-keyframe mask; guidance + imputation at inference
Novelty: One unified model handling arbitrary dense-or-sparse keyframe placement + PARTIAL per-joint keyframe constraints, jointly with text
Result: — (precise diverse motions to flexible keyframes)
Others: Steered by keyframes (dense/sparse), partial per-joint constraints, text
Evaluation Methods: HumanML3D keyframe-conditioned; guidance vs imputation

Article: Motion Synthesis with Sparse and Flexible Keyjoint Control (SFControl)
Author: Inwoo Hwang et al. (Seoul National Univ.)
Method: Decomposed diffusion: synthesize keyjoint trajectories from sparse signals, then full-body motion from the completed keyjoints
Novelty: TIME-AGNOSTIC sparse-keyjoint control (no frame-specific timing annotations) — animator-friendly
Others: Steered by sparse keyjoint positions without timing — more flexible than dense trajectories
Evaluation Methods: — (experiments across datasets; protocol not in abstract)

Article: SMooDi: Stylized Motion Diffusion Model
Author: Lei Zhong et al. (Northeastern)
Method: Adapt a pretrained text-to-motion diffusion model for stylization via style guidance + a lightweight style adaptor steering toward a reference style
Novelty: Jointly generates across broad CONTENT (text) and diverse STYLE (reference) in one diffusion model
Result: Stylized T2M FID 1.609 (vs 6.127 MLD+Motion Puzzle); SRA 72.42% (vs 63.77%)
Models: Latent diffusion (MLD) + style adaptor + style guidance
Others: Steered by content text + a reference style sequence — style control (relevant to abstract 'look menacing')
Evaluation Methods: HumanML3D+100STYLE: content (R-Prec), style (SRA), realism (FID)

Article: Taming Diffusion Probabilistic Models for Character Control (CAMDM)
Author: Rui Chen et al. (Tencent AI Lab; Komura, Tan)
Method: A transformer Conditional Autoregressive Motion Diffusion Model: given motion history + coarse user control, generate diverse future motion in real time
Novelty: First diffusion model for REAL-TIME interactive character control from high-level user input; one unified multi-style model
Result: — (real-time, high-quality, diverse, multi-style)
Others: Steered ONLINE by joystick-style high-level control (direction/speed) + style — closest to real-time COMMAND of generative motion
Evaluation Methods: — (qualitative real-time control demos)

Article: Text-Guided Synthesis of Crowd Animation
Author: Xuebo Ji et al. (HKU; LightSpeed Studios)
Method: LLM canonicalizes a text script into per-group sentences; two conditional latent-diffusion models predict agent start/goal + guiding velocity fields; a local RVO navigator drives the groups
Novelty: First pipeline for language-guided generation of environment-compatible crowd scenarios with many agents navigating in real time
Others: CLOSEST crowd analog: language -> real-time group/formation crowd control
Evaluation Methods: Qualitative over 8 environments incl. unseen + novel descriptions

Article: Continuous Locomotive Crowd Behavior Generation (CrowdES)
Author: Inhwan Bae et al. (GIST)
Method: A diffusion 'Crowd Emitter' continually spawns individuals with per-agent attributes; a 'Crowd Simulator' produces long-term locomotion + a Markov chain for diverse actions
Metric: Scene-level population dynamics + individual trajectory accuracy (proposed benchmark)
Novelty: Generates continuous, ongoing crowd behaviour (not a snapshot) with heterogeneous, user-controllable agents
Others: Scene-level controllable crowd generation; continuous-in-time + a realism benchmark
Evaluation Methods: Scene + individual realism benchmark; cross-environment generalization

Article: CEDRL: Simulating Diverse Crowds with Example-Driven Deep Reinforcement Learning
Author: Andreas Panayiotou et al. (Univ. Cyprus / CYENS)
Method: Deep RL leveraging multiple real crowd datasets, with curriculum (shrinking observation space) + parametric task-typed reward functions to learn diverse abstract behaviours
Metric: Generalization; a behavioural-complexity metric; vs other simulators
Novelty: Example-driven multi-dataset RL learning a broad behaviour spectrum without large labels + a complexity metric
Models: RL policy with a complexity-weight parameter
Others: RL for crowd BEHAVIOUR (not physics locomotion) — the RL-on-our-turf crowd line; real-time controllability
Evaluation Methods: Seen/unseen real-data comparison + controllability via complexity weight

Article: GREIL-Crowds: Crowd Simulation with Deep Reinforcement Learning and Examples
Author: Panayiotis Charalambous et al. (CYENS / Inria / UPC)
Method: Deep RL guided by reference crowd data; reward keeps agents in 'comfortable' states defined by distributions (e.g. neighbour spacing) from real crowds
Novelty: Example-guided RL reward from real-crowd comfort distributions; reproduces goal-seeking, grouping, wandering
Others: Foundational data-driven RL crowd-behaviour paper; emergent group coordination from data (predecessor to CEDRL)
Evaluation Methods: Behaviour reproduction vs reference real crowds

Article: Gen-C: Populating Virtual Worlds with Generative Crowds
Author: Andreas Panayiotou et al. (CYENS / Clemson)
Method: A dual Variational Graph Autoencoder, conditioned on text via LLMs, generates crowd scenarios as time-expanded graphs encoding agent actions + interactions
Novelty: Generative crowd framework via time-expanded graph + dual-VGAE capturing high-level decisions + interactions, text-conditioned
Others: Language/text-conditioned scene-level crowd authoring with interaction modelling
Evaluation Methods: Qualitative across diverse scenarios

Article: Emergent Crowds Dynamics from Language-Driven Multi-Agent Interactions
Author: Yibo Liu et al. (Univ. Victoria)
Method: Decentralized agent-based crowd where each agent uses an LLM for dialogue + language-driven navigation from perception, personality, emotion, relationships
Novelty: LLM-controlled per-agent dialogue + navigation with personality/emotion conditioning -> emergent grouping/ungrouping
Others: Language-conditioned DECENTRALIZED crowd coordination; agents negotiate via dialogue
Evaluation Methods: Two scenarios showing emergent grouping/ungrouping

Article: CrowdVLA: Embodied Vision-Language-Action Agents for Context-Aware Crowd Simulation
Author: Juyeong Hwang et al.
Method: VLA agents interpret scene semantics from vision + language; a LoRA-fine-tuned VLM maps perception to motion-skill actions with exploration-based QA
Novelty: Shifts crowd sim from geometry/collision-avoidance to perception-driven, consequence-aware decision making
Others: Language-instructed perception-driven crowd agents; brings the VLA paradigm (cf. Diao's pi0 pointer) into crowds
Evaluation Methods: — (not detailed)

Article: Understanding complex crowd dynamics with generative neural simulators (NeCS)
Author: Koen Minartz et al. (TU Eindhoven)
Method: A data-driven Neural Crowd Simulator trained on large real pedestrian data learning N-body interactions; used as a controllable surrogate
Novelty: Generative neural surrogate giving lab-like controllability at the statistical resolution of real data; recovers many-body avoidance
Others: Learned (not rule-based) collective crowd dynamics from data — the generative-simulator angle
Evaluation Methods: Validation vs known statistical/physical crowd features

Article: RESCUE: Crowd Evacuation Simulation via Controlling SDM-United Characters
Author: Xiaolin Liu et al. (Tianjin Univ. / Cardiff)
Method: Real-time evacuation coupling a 3D-adaptive Social Force Model decision mechanism with personalized gait/motion control along a sensory-decision-motor pipeline
Metric: Visual realism; evacuation analysis (part-level force viz)
Novelty: Unifies crowd-level decision (Social Force) with character-level personalized gait in one real-time system
Others: Real-time multi-agent crowd coordination at behaviour+motion level (group decision drives individual gait)
Evaluation Methods: Trajectory-planning validation + visual plausibility

Article: Generating Diverse and Natural 3D Human Motions from Text (T2M / HumanML3D)
Author: Chuan Guo et al. (Li Cheng's group, U Alberta)
Method: Temporal VAE with a text2length module; motion-snippet codes generated progressively from text
Metric: R-Precision, FID, MM-Dist, Diversity, MultiModality (suite introduced here)
Novelty: Introduces HumanML3D (14k+ motions, 45k descriptions) and the metric suite that became the field's standard benchmark
Result: The benchmark and protocol nearly every later text-to-motion paper evaluates on
Others: Foundations row (added 2026-07-02): grounds the real-time line in theme 5
Evaluation Methods: HumanML3D / KIT-ML protocol (their own, now standard)

Article: TM2T: Stochastic and Tokenized Modeling for the Reciprocal Generation of 3D Human Motions and Texts
Author: Chuan Guo et al. (Li Cheng's group, U Alberta)
Method: Discretizes motion into VQ tokens; text<->motion treated as two-way neural translation, jointly trained
Novelty: First VQ tokenization of motion for generation - the token-based line (T2M-GPT, MotionGPT, MoMask) starts here
Result: Token-based generation competitive in both directions (text->motion and motion->text)
Others: The discretization Motion BPE presupposes
Evaluation Methods: HumanML3D protocol

Article: MDM: Human Motion Diffusion Model
Author: Guy Tevet et al.
Method: Classifier-free diffusion transformer over raw motion predicting the clean sample, with geometric losses
Novelty: The lightweight diffusion baseline of the field
Result: Quality reference that the real-time distillation line (MotionLCM etc.) accelerates
Evaluation Methods: HumanML3D protocol

Article: MotionDiffuse: Text-Driven Human Motion Generation with Diffusion Model
Author: Mingyuan Zhang et al.
Method: Large diffusion model with cross-modality linear transformer; body-part-level control, arbitrary length
Novelty: First large diffusion text-to-motion model; with MDM, the root of the diffusion branch
Result: Established diffusion-quality text-to-motion at scale
Evaluation Methods: HumanML3D protocol

Article: T2M-GPT: Generating Human Motion from Textual Descriptions with Discrete Representations
Author: Jianrong Zhang et al.
Method: VQ-VAE (EMA + code reset) + GPT generating motion-token sequences from CLIP text features
Novelty: The plain tokenize-then-LM recipe matching diffusion quality - the minimal instance of the pipeline Motion BPE extends
Result: Its VQ tokens are the 'raw VQ' baseline in Motion BPE's key ablation
Evaluation Methods: HumanML3D protocol

Article: MotionGPT: Human Motion as a Foreign Language
Author: Biao Jiang et al.
Method: Motion VQ tokens folded into a pretrained T5 vocabulary; unified motion-language pretraining + instruction tuning
Novelty: One LLM for generation, captioning, and prediction - motion as a 'foreign language'
Result: Motion BPE's named differentiator target: it stops at fixed-length VQ codes, no BPE layer
Evaluation Methods: HumanML3D protocol

Article: MoMask: Generative Masked Modeling of 3D Human Motions
Author: Chuan Guo et al. (Li Cheng's group, U Alberta)
Method: Residual VQ (base + refinement code layers) + masked generative transformer with iterative parallel decoding
Novelty: High-quality reference on HumanML3D; faster than autoregressive decoding
Result: The plan's named P5 basis ('the MoMask line'); residual VQ is the upgrade path for Motion BPE's tokenizer milestone
Evaluation Methods: HumanML3D protocol

Article: TEMOS: Generating Diverse Human Motions from Textual Descriptions
Author: Mathis Petrovich et al.
Method: Transformer VAE with a joint motion-text latent space; SMPL-compatible output
Novelty: The main pre-diffusion VAE baseline for diverse text-conditioned generation
Result: Pre-diffusion diversity baseline
Evaluation Methods: KIT-ML protocol
